title: Cross-Platform Locator Strategy
context: Android and iOS use different attribute names for the same UI concept (e.g., Android uses @text while iOS uses @label or @name). Scripts that hardcode platform-specific locators in shared Keywords break when switching platforms, requiring duplicate test code.

problem.description: Platform-specific locators in shared Keywords make scripts non-portable between Android and iOS.
symptoms: Tests pass on Android but fail on iOS with 'Element not found'
Duplicate test files maintained separately per platform
Locator breaks after OS upgrade changes attribute names

antipattern.description: Hardcoding Android-only @text attribute in a shared Keyword
antipattern.why_bad: iOS does not have @text attribute — the same locator silently fails on iOS

solution.description: Use a priority-based locator strategy. Start with the most portable option and fall back only when necessary.

steps.name: Unified Locator Priority
steps.description: Follow this priority order: 1) Accessibility ID — works natively on both platforms. 2) ID — usually stable but may be platform-prefixed on Android. 3) Smart XPath — use only as fallback.

steps.name: Smart XPath Pattern
steps.description: When you must find by visible text, combine all platform attributes in one XPath expression.
code: # Handles Android (@text) and iOS (@label, @name) in one expression

steps.name: Page Object Encapsulation
steps.description: Keep all locators inside Page Object resource files. The Test layer should only call semantic Keywords, never reference locators directly.
code: # tests/LoginTest.robot — test layer never sees locators

ai_instruction: If a locator is not provided, prioritize searching for Accessibility ID. If analysis shows different locators for the same feature on different platforms, create a Unified Mapping Table in the implementation plan before writing any Keywords.
